topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of old paper and forgotten lavender. I was supposed to be looking for holiday decorations, but my fingers had snagged on a brittle, leather-bound box tucked behind a stack of mildewed photo albums. Inside, nestled amongst yellowed tissue paper, was a meticulous collection. Every single assignment from my entire school career, neatly cataloged in manila folders. My breath hitched. I pulled one out—a geometry proof from sophomore year. The handwriting was my mother’s, elegant and precise. A sudden, hot flush crept up my neck, and I quickly shoved the folder under my arm, suddenly desperate to flee the suffocating quiet of the space. How could she have kept *everything*? It felt like an invasion, a sudden, overwhelming exposure of every clumsy effort I’d ever made.

[story 2]

I found the stash tucked inside a seldom-used desk drawer in the spare room. It was a trove: essays on Shakespeare, painstakingly researched history reports, even the messy, incomplete science projects from middle school. As I ran my thumb over the crisp edges of a history paper, a strange tightening settled in my chest, like a knot tightening around my ribs. I stood there for a long moment, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. *She saved all of it.* The realization hit me with a dull thud. I started pacing the small room, my gaze darting from folder to folder, unable to settle on any single item. Every completed task seemed to illuminate a gap in my own memory, highlighting every moment I’d felt inadequate. I backed away slowly, feeling the need to hide the evidence, as if these papers were somehow judging my entire academic history.

[story 3]

It was a startling discovery. While cleaning out the basement, I stumbled upon a large, sealed container labeled simply, "Schoolwork." Inside, the sheer volume was staggering—hundreds of assignments, tests, and projects, all preserved with surprising care. My hands trembled slightly as I lifted a stack of early English literature essays. The sheer volume of documented effort made my stomach clench. I wanted to drop them all, to erase the tangible proof of every perceived shortfall. I retreated to the hallway, pressing my back against the cool plaster wall. I kept glancing back at the box, feeling scrutinized by the silent collection of completed work. The weight of it all—the constant, unspoken record of my performance—made my shoulders slump forward, heavy with an unfamiliar sense of exposure.
